title: Hydration
summary: Hydration prevents dehydration and can make thick secretions easier to manage, but drinking beyond thirst does not flush out viruses or dramatically shorten a cold. The goal is normal hydration, not forced litres of fluid.
evidenceSnapshot: Strong physiological rationale for replacing fluid losses; limited direct evidence that extra fluid changes cold duration.

interventions.name: Water and usual fluids
interventions.finding: Maintains circulation, urine output and mucosal moisture.
interventions.practical: Drink to thirst and take extra small amounts with fever, sweating, vomiting or poor intake.
interventions.cautions: People with heart failure, kidney disease or fluid restrictions need individual advice.

interventions.name: Warm drinks and soup
interventions.finding: Can soothe the throat, provide fluid and make nasal airflow feel easier.
interventions.practical: Choose low-salt options when sodium restriction matters.
interventions.mechanism: Warmth and sensory effects may reduce discomfort.
interventions.cautions: Very salty soups can be unsuitable for some heart or kidney conditions.

interventions.name: Oral rehydration solution
interventions.finding: Useful when vomiting, diarrhoea or poor intake causes meaningful fluid and electrolyte loss.
interventions.practical: Use a commercial oral rehydration product mixed exactly as directed.
interventions.mechanism: Provides glucose and electrolytes in a ratio that supports intestinal absorption.
interventions.cautions: Sports drinks and juice are not equivalent for significant dehydration.

interventions.name: Excessive forced water
interventions.finding: Does not cure infection and can rarely cause dangerous low sodium.
interventions.practical: Use thirst, urine colour and output as guides rather than a fixed high target.
interventions.cautions: Higher risk in endurance athletes, kidney disease and people taking some medicines.

practicalSteps: Aim for pale-yellow urine and regular urination.
Offer small frequent drinks rather than a large glass when nauseated.
For children, count wet nappies and watch tears, mouth moisture and alertness.
Add fluid through ice blocks, soup or fruit when plain water is unappealing.
avoidOrCare: Severe dehydration may require urgent medical treatment, not just more home fluids.
Dry mouth alone can also result from mouth breathing or medicines.

myths.claim: Eight or more glasses will flush the virus out.
myths.reality: Normal hydration supports body function; the immune system clears the virus.
Milk can leave a temporary coating sensation but has not been shown to increase respiratory mucus production.

redFlags: Sunken eyes, cold hands/feet, fast breathing or fast heart rate
Confusion, marked drowsiness or inability to drink
Infant with fewer wet nappies, no tears or sunken fontanelle
unsettled: Trials have not established an ideal fluid volume specifically for uncomplicated colds.
